did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a deep learning algorithm, specifically a fine-tuned Microsoft ResNet-152 convolutional neural network (CNN), to classify clinical images of 12 benign and malignant cutaneous tumors (including basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and melanoma). The model was trained on 19,398 images from the Asan, MED-NODE, and atlas site datasets and validated against the Asan, Hallym, and Edinburgh datasets to achieve diagnostic performance comparable to dermatologists.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' and presents original experimental results. It describes the development, training (using 19,398 images from Asan, MED-NODE, and atlas datasets), and validation (using Asan, Hallym, and Edinburgh datasets) of a deep learning algorithm (Microsoft ResNet-152) for classifying 12 cutaneous tumors. The study includes specific methodology, quantitative results (AUC, sensitivity, specificity), and a comparison with dermatologists, which are characteristic of primary research rather than a review.

title: Classification of the Clinical Images for Benign and Malignant Cutaneous Tumors Using a Deep Learning Algorithm
brief_summary: This study evaluated a deep learning algorithm, specifically a fine-tuned Microsoft ResNet-152 convolutional neural network, for classifying clinical images of 12 benign and malignant cutaneous tumors. The model was trained on 19,398 images from the Asan, MED-NODE, and atlas site datasets and validated using testing portions of the Asan, Hallym, and Edinburgh datasets. The primary evaluation approach involved calculating the area under the curve (AUC), sensitivity, and specificity for each tumor type, with a comparative performance analysis against 16 dermatologists using 480 test images. Key results showed high diagnostic accuracy, with AUCs for basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, intraepithelial carcinoma, and melanoma ranging from 0.82 to 0.96 across datasets. The algorithm's performance was found to be comparable to that of the dermatologists, with superior performance in diagnosing basal cell carcinoma and melanocytic nevus in specific datasets. The authors concluded that while the AI system is effective, future improvements require additional images with broader age and ethnic diversity.

all_task_types.target: 12 discrete cutaneous tumor classes (basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, intraepithelial carcinoma, actinic keratosis, seborrheic keratosis, malignant melanoma, melanocytic nevus, lentigo, pyogenic granuloma, hemangioma, dermatofibroma, and wart)
all_task_types.details: The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories for 12 skin diseases using a fine-tuned Microsoft ResNet-152 convolutional neural network. Performance was evaluated using Area Under the Curve (AUC), sensitivity, and specificity for each class. The system outputs probabilities for each class, and predictions are made by comparing these outputs against a threshold. Top-1 accuracy was also reported.

datasets_used.name: Asan dataset
datasets_used.description: A private dataset collected from the Department of Dermatology at Asan Medical Center via retrospective chart review. It contains clinical images taken from 2000 through 2016. The study utilized a subset of 17,125 images from 4,867 patients for the 12 target disorders, and an additional 'additional Asan dataset' of 159,477 images from 248 other diseases for specificity training.

use.training: Used for training the ResNet-152 model (17,125 images).
use.other: An additional subset of 159,477 images from 248 other diseases was used to improve specificity.

datasets_used.name: MED-NODE dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the University Medical Center Groningen, containing melanoma and nevus images.

use.training: Used for training the ResNet-152 model.

datasets_used.name: Atlas site dataset
datasets_used.description: A collection of images downloaded from several public dermatologic atlas websites (e.g., dermquest.com, dermatlas.net, dermis.net, etc.).
classes_used: Various cutaneous tumors (specific classes not enumerated, but used to supplement training data)

use.training: Used for training the ResNet-152 model.

datasets_used.name: Hallym dataset
datasets_used.description: A private dataset from Hallym University College of Medicine, consisting of Basal Cell Carcinoma cases.

datasets_used.name: Edinburgh dataset (Edinburgh Dermofit Image Library)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset obtained from the Edinburgh Dermofit Image Library, consisting of images of 10 disorders.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the diagnostic performance of the deep learning algorithm for classifying 12 cutaneous tumors across different datasets (Asan, Edinburgh, Hallym).
To assess the diagnostic accuracy of the model, including analysis over a range of thresholds (0.0000 to 1.0000) to determine optimal thresholds that maximize the sum of sensitivity and specificity.
To visualize and compare the prediction of malignancy for the algorithm versus 16 dermatologists using 480 randomly chosen images from the Asan and Edinburgh datasets.
To measure the rate at which the model yields the correct label with its top one prediction for a given image.
To provide visual explanations of the predictions made by the CNN and visualize the features selected by it (e.g., coarse and irregular portions of lesions for malignancy).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using Area Under the Curve (AUC), sensitivity, specificity, and top-1 accuracy, with qualitative comparisons stating the algorithm's performance was 'comparable' to that of dermatologists.

Asan Test Dataset.notes: Top-1 accuracy was 57.3 ± 0.9%. The model was trained on 19,398 images total (Asan, MED-NODE, atlas). Sensitivity and specificity were analyzed over a threshold range of 0.0000 to 1.0000.

Edinburgh Dataset.notes: Top-1 accuracy was 55.7 ± 1.5%. Lentigo and Wart were not included in the Edinburgh dataset results. The accuracy for BCC and melanoma was lower than in the Asan dataset, possibly due to ethnicity differences (Caucasian vs Asian).

Hallym Dataset.notes: The Hallym dataset consisted of BCC cases alone. Sensitivity was analyzed over a threshold range of 0.0000 to 1.0000. Results were comparable to the Asan dataset.

Comparison with Dermatologists.notes: Evaluated on 480 randomly chosen images from the Asan (260 images) and Edinburgh (220 images) datasets. The algorithm's performance was comparable to that of 16 dermatologists (10 professors and 6 clinicians). In the dermatologist test, sensitivity for BCC and melanocytic nevus cases with the Edinburgh dataset was low (< 40%).

Pilot Study (Asan-only training).notes: This pilot study highlights the impact of training data diversity. Adding the atlas dataset (1,561 BCC images) improved BCC classification results.

training_details.preprocessing: Lesion of interest was cropped and resized to 224x224 pixels
training_details.optimization_details: Details available in Supplementary Materials and Methods (not explicitly reported in main text)

classification_or_output_head.decision_rule: If any output exceeded a specific threshold, the disorder was retrieved as a differential diagnosis

preprocessing_techniques: Images were resized to 224 x 224 pixels
conclusions: The deep learning algorithm (Microsoft ResNet-152) achieved high diagnostic performance for classifying 12 benign and malignant cutaneous tumors, with AUCs ranging from 0.82 to 0.96 across different datasets.
The algorithm's performance on 480 test images from the Asan and Edinburgh datasets was comparable to that of 16 dermatologists.
The model demonstrated superior performance in diagnosing basal cell carcinoma and melanocytic nevus in specific datasets compared to dermatologists.

Performance varied across datasets, with lower accuracy for basal cell carcinoma and melanoma in the Edinburgh dataset compared to the Asan dataset, potentially due to ethnic differences (Caucasian vs. Asian) and skin color variations.
The algorithm can be tuned to match specific clinical settings, such as prioritizing high sensitivity for screening malignancy.
To improve performance and generalizability, additional images with a broader range of ages and ethnicities should be collected.

The study was limited to the classification of 12 specific skin disorders, and expanding the repertoire of skin images to include other cutaneous tumors and normal skin types is necessary to reduce false-positive rates in real clinical practice.
important_remarks: The study is limited to the classification of only 12 specific cutaneous tumors, which may not reflect the complexity of real-world clinical practice involving other skin conditions.

Performance varied significantly across datasets due to ethnic differences; the model trained primarily on Asian patients (Asan dataset) showed lower accuracy for Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) and Melanoma when tested on a Caucasian population (Edinburgh dataset), highlighting a potential bias in generalizability.
The Asan dataset contained a disproportionate number of acral lentiginous melanomas (69.6%), which may skew the model's learning for melanoma compared to other subtypes more common in Caucasians.
The study lacked formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests; performance comparisons with dermatologists were qualitative ('comparable') rather than statistically rigorous.
The algorithm's performance was evaluated against only 16 dermatologists, which is a small sample size for establishing robust clinical equivalence.

Korean dermatologists in the comparison group had low sensitivity (<40%) for BCC and melanocytic nevus in the Edinburgh dataset, likely because they are accustomed to Asian skin presentations, suggesting the comparison group itself had a demographic bias.
The study relied on retrospective chart reviews and public datasets, which may introduce selection bias and does not account for prospective clinical workflow integration.
The model was designed to output differential diagnoses rather than a single classification, which changes the interpretation of 'accuracy' and requires different clinical validation strategies.
Data leakage risks are present as the training data included images from public atlas websites, which may have overlapping or non-independent sources compared to the test sets.
The study did not report on the model's performance across a broad range of skin types (Fitzpatrick scale) or ages, limiting the assessment of fairness and equity in diagnosis.

The pilot study demonstrated that adding diverse training data (atlas dataset) significantly improved BCC classification, underscoring the critical importance of dataset diversity for model robustness.
